metadata.summary: A specification answers WHAT to build and WHY, functioning as the anti-hallucination anchor for LLM execution agents with structured functional requirements and acceptance criteria.

content.title: Writing Specifications
content.summary: A specification answers WHAT to build and WHY. It functions as the anti-hallucination anchor for LLM execution agents: every FR-X must be a SHALL statement meeting SMART criteria, and every acceptance criterion must use concrete values in Given-When-Then format. The spec is not considered complete until a second, fresh-context agent outputs "SPEC APPROVED" after running the AC coverage checklist (happy path, error handling, boundary conditions, security, performance, accessibility).

why.p: Traditional specs were alignment artifacts for humans. LLM agents require specs that act as programming interfaces: structured requirements with measurable thresholds replace narrative context and implied constraints. Without an explicit "Out of Scope" section, agents implement out-of-scope features. The "curse of instructions" — model adherence drops with more than 200 FRs — forces ruthless prioritization and phase splitting.

list.item: Before any LLM-assisted implementation: the spec is the primary context document
When requirements come as vague requests — transform them into structured FR-X + AC-X artifacts
Writing CLAUDE.md or project rules using the three-tier boundary system (Always / Ask First / Never)
Any feature affecting multiple files, teams, or external APIs
Bug fix with a known root cause and clear fix — write the fix, not a spec
Configuration-only change — no functional requirements to specify
One-off script or throwaway prototype — overhead exceeds value
When requirements are so volatile they will change before implementation starts

rule.statement: A specification answers WHAT to build and WHY; it must not answer HOW.
rule.rationale: Conflating WHAT with HOW produces a document that confuses intent with implementation, prevents clean design alternatives, and creates traceability problems when designs change.

list.item: Specification: WHAT and WHY → requirements, user stories, acceptance criteria
Design doc: HOW → architecture, API contracts, data models
Implementation plan: IN WHAT ORDER → tasks, dependencies, token estimates

rule.statement: The specification is the anti-hallucination anchor: every LLM execution agent loads it as its primary context document.
rule.rationale: Without a spec, agents infer intent from code patterns and produce implementations that diverge from stakeholder intent. The spec is the source of truth for what gets built.

section.name: six-core-areas-for-llm-execution
section.p: Research across 2,500+ agent configurations identified six areas that must be explicit in a spec for reliable LLM execution:

list.item: Commands: executable commands with flags (e.g., "npm test", "pytest -v")
Testing: framework, location, and coverage target
Project structure: directory layout with exact paths
Code style: real code snippets showing the actual pattern, not prose descriptions
Git workflow: branch naming and commit format
Boundaries: what agents must never touch

rule.statement: Every spec must include an explicit Boundaries section with Always, Ask First, and Never tiers.
rule.rationale: Without explicit boundaries, agents default to maximum autonomy. The "Out of Scope" section and "Never" tier are the most important boundary setters — without them, agents implement excluded features.

list.item: Always (Safe Actions): run tests before committing, follow existing patterns, add type annotations
Ask First (High Impact): database schema changes, API contract modifications, dependency upgrades
Never (Hard Stops): commit secrets, delete production data, bypass security checks

rule.statement: Replace every vague qualifier with a concrete, measurable value.
rule.rationale: Agents interpret vague terms differently from humans and from each other. "Fast" produces implementations targeting anywhere from 50ms to 5s; "<500ms p95" is unambiguous.

list.item: "Should be fast" → "Response time <500ms p95"
"Handle errors gracefully" → "Display error message, log to Sentry, no stack traces to user"
"Support many users" → "10,000 concurrent connections"
"Good user experience" → "<3 clicks to complete task"
"Secure authentication" → "bcrypt with 12 rounds, JWT with 15min expiry"
"Mobile friendly" → "Responsive breakpoints: 320px, 768px, 1024px"

rule.statement: Keep total FR count below 200; divide large specs into phases when FR count exceeds that threshold.
rule.rationale: The "curse of instructions" — model adherence drops as the total number of instructions increases. Ruthless prioritization and phase splitting maintain agent reliability.

list.item: Use table format for dense information instead of prose
Reference external files instead of inlining full content
Include only relevant code snippets, not entire files
Divide large specs into backend/frontend/migration phases with explicit boundaries

rule.statement: Every FR must satisfy SMART: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound (priority-assigned).
rule.rationale: Non-SMART requirements are unverifiable. An agent cannot determine whether "users need auth" is implemented; it can verify "unregistered users cannot create accounts."

list.item: Specific: exactly who and what (bad: "Users need auth" → good: "Unregistered users cannot create accounts")
Measurable: how to measure success (bad: "Should be fast" → good: "Login <500ms p95")
Achievable: technically feasible (bad: "AI reads minds" → good: "OAuth 2.0 with Google")
Relevant: tied to business goal (bad: "Nice to have" → good: "Blocks monetization")
Time-bound: priority assigned (bad: "Eventually" → good: "Must have for MVP")

rule.statement: Use "System SHALL" for must-have requirements; use "System SHOULD" for should-have; never use passive voice or conditional language.
rule.rationale: SHALL is unambiguous obligation. Conditional language ("should", "could", "may") allows agents to skip requirements under their own interpretation of priority.

example.code:
### FR-001: Email Registration
**Requirement:** System SHALL allow users to register with email and password. **Rationale:** Enables user identification for premium features. **Traces to:** US-001 **Priority:** Must **Validation Rules:** - Email: RFC 5322 format, unique in system - Password: min 8 chars, 1 uppercase, 1 digit
example.note: Traces to a user story. Priority is explicit. Validation rules are concrete, not prose.

list.item: Must: MVP requirement, cannot launch without
Should: important feature, include if possible
Could: nice to have, only if time allows
Won't: explicit exclusion, not in this release

rule.statement: Every FR must have at least one AC in Given-When-Then format with concrete, specific values — no vague terms.
rule.rationale: ACs produced by agents are often too happy-path. Error handling, boundary conditions, and security scenarios require explicit prompting. A spec without these ACs produces implementations that handle only the success case.

example.code:
### AC-001: Successful Registration
Given: User is on registration page And: Email "test@example.com" does not exist in system When: User enters email "test@example.com" and password "SecurePass1" And: User clicks "Register" button Then: Account is created in database And: User receives verification email within 30 seconds And: User is redirected to dashboard And: Success message "Check your email" is displayed
example.note: Concrete values throughout: specific email, specific password, specific timeout (30 seconds), specific redirect destination, specific message text.

rule.statement: Every feature must have ACs covering: happy path, error handling, boundary conditions, security scenarios, performance scenarios (if NFRs exist), and accessibility.
rule.rationale: Without explicit coverage requirements, agents write only happy-path ACs. Security scenarios (auth bypass, injection attempts) are almost never included without explicit instruction.

list.item: Happy path: main success scenario with expected outputs
Error handling: validation failures, service unavailability, timeout
Boundary conditions: min/max values, empty states, null inputs
Security scenarios: auth bypass attempt, injection attempt
Performance scenarios: load handling, response time under concurrency
Accessibility: screen reader, keyboard navigation

template.purpose: Full feature spec with user personas, FRs, NFRs, ACs, boundaries
Minimal viable spec for simple single-task features
REST/GraphQL API endpoint spec with request/response/error shapes
UI component spec with props, variants, states, accessibility
Prompts for writing and reviewing specs for LLM executability
Shell script validating FR-X, AC-X, Given/When/Then presence and flagging vague terms
